text: The surgeon encounters adhesions and thickening of the gallbladder wall during a laparoscopic cholecystectomy. Electrocautery and a Maryland dissector are used for both blunt and sharp dissection. Hydro dissection is also employed to manage the adhesions. The gallbladder is grasped proximally and pulled upwards to clarify the anatomy in the neck region, with traction applied towards the right shoulder to highlight structures in the Calot's triangle.

The surgeon uses a 5mm sucker to perform hydro dissection around the gallbladder neck and Calot's triangle, avoiding electrocautery due to dense adhesions and increased vascularity. The water flow helps clarify anatomical planes, reducing the risk of injury to vital structures and bleeding.

The surgeon performs gradual adenolysis at the junction of the cystic duct and gallbladder neck, using the tip of a sucker to create a plane between the cystic artery and duct. The cystic duct appears dilated. Electrocautery is used to address small bends not amenable to blunt dissection. The gallbladder neck is lifted above the sylovial sulcus, providing clearer exposure of Hartman's pouch. The cystic duct is further skeletonized, revealing an injury caused by the tip of a hook. The surgeon clips the cystic artery to improve visibility and coagulates its distal part.

The surgeon carefully monitors the tip of the hook during electrocautery to avoid further injury. A pinpoint iatrogenic injury is identified in the middle part of the cystic duct. The surgeon performs further dissection and cyclinitization of the cystic duct using Maryland forceps and blunt dissection to clarify the anatomy. A suspicious structure, likely peritoneum, is reflected over the right side of the cystic duct. The surgeon creates space in the groove of this reflection to gain more proximal length and a clearer view of the cystic duct. An artery with pulsations and two branches is observed, indicating variable anatomy.

The surgeon dissects the peritoneal attachments on both medial and lateral aspects of the gallbladder to clarify the anatomy of a variable artery. Clips are applied to the prominent right-sided branch of the artery, which is skeletonized using hydro dissection. The vessel is clipped proximally and distally before being cut, revealing its lumen. Clips are then applied to the cystic duct, with two proximal clips and one distal clip placed near the gallbladder neck. The cystic duct is cut between the second and third clips, at the site of a previous iatrogenic injury. The gallbladder is separated from the liver bed using hydro dissection or electrocautery.

The surgeon uses spray mode and intermittent pulsatile current with electrocautery to minimize smoke production while dissecting the gallbladder from the liver bed. The gallbladder is gradually freed from its last attachment to the liver, and a grasper is used to remove it through the epigastric port. A drain is placed inside the surgical site for postoperative management.
